speaker: interviewer
text: Mother after she found you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I went to visit her, and she was married. I only went for a holiday. I only stood maybe a week. And I came back to Paris because I worked. I worked as an electrician, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for Citroen. And in Citroen I-- I-- I worked for-- till I actually found my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met my wife-- have you got-- I tell you. Well, one time in France, in Paris, July the 14th is a very, very big holiday. And this holiday I went to speak to people in a park. And so I came down, and I spoke to the people my experiences from the camp and war and so. And then it became about noontime, and I was finished with the speeches, and the people went home to celebrate and to have to eat and everything. And I stayed in the park, and I was reading my paper, and-- because I had nobody. All I had was a neighbor cat what came to visit me all the time. And when I-- while I was sitting there, and it became a little darker, and the lights came on-- the garden lights. And the place was called Place de la Republique in Paris. And so I took my chair, and I moved to the light. And I was sitting there reading the paper, and there were a bunch of girls and they spoke Russian and Jewish and they came. There were about six or seven of them. And they were sitting at the bench beside me. There were also benches and chairs. I was sitting in a chair and then the bench. The girls were sitting on the chair-- on the bench.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I listened to them for a little while and so I said to one girl, I said, how long are you here from Russia? And she said, oh, we just came just a couple of months or three months ago or something like that. And, oh, I said, yeah, I was born in Moscow. But I left in 1928, and I would like to know how is Moscow and Russia going there now? You know [INAUDIBLE] So we started talking and talking, and the other girls, they said, OK, are you coming? They go dancing. Are you coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that girl said, no, I'm not actually coming. I'm going to wait for a cousin of hers. So she stayed with me, and we talked and we talked and we talked and we talked, and it was 2:00 in the morning and I brought her home. And so I asked her if I could see her again. And she said, oh, yeah, maybe next Saturday when I'm not working. I said fine. So I went to visit her next Saturday, next Saturday, and now it is 14th of July will be 49 years we're married.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. [LAUGHS] It's wonderful. What have you done since you came to Canada? When did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Canada in 1951. Well, in Paris, we had a choice what we wanted to be. I didn't want to be a doctor because I knew already how that looks like. I didn't want to be a dentist because I knew I-- I cannot work in somebody else's mouth. So I figured what else am I going to do? Maybe-- maybe I'm going to stick to that what saved my life. I going to stick to electricity. That saved my life, and I stick with it. And so when I came to Canada, I said I'm electrician. I have some French papers, and I showed them the papers. And I was actually specialized. I learned a little bit more in Paris. So I became an electrician, and they gave me papers, and I worked for the CPR. And I worked for the CPR about six years, and then I stopped, and I went in all kind of businesses. I tried out to do businesses. We went into restaurant business, and I went into real estate and but I'm too good of a nature. I-- I help more people than I can make money. And so I say-- my wife said, you are no good businessman. So I said fine, I go back as an electrician. I went back to the CPR, and that's where I retired as an electronic engineer. I-- everything I learned by myself from books.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. And two of your sons are working for CP.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both my sons are working in the CPR. They are electricians.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's wonderful. It's almost a family business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's a beautiful story. You referred to luck many times during your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think made you lucky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heh. Well, I will have to tell you the truth, I'm not religious, and I-- I do not believe in actual luck. It's just an expression. Why did he choose me to be his electrician assistant? And later on, he taught me everything about electricity, and I was his boss. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you call that luck or it's just circumstantial, and that's how it went all my life. [LAUGHS] And, well, I'm 72 years old, and all I need is once in a while an aspirin or once in awhile I get the heartburn, and I take baking soda with a little bit of water, and this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] It's 52 years after the war and after the Holocaust, and I know you speak to students at the college.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you give to future generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I give them a message that it is not good to hate. Absolutely devastating for the person who hates and for the person who is hated. And it will bring actually to nothing. It will not benefit the person who hates, and it will not benefit the person what is hated. This is the worst thing in the world is to hate. And it doesn't matter what you hate. If you hate somebody with blond hair and blue eyes or if you hate somebody with a crooked foot, it doesn't matter. Hate is the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your message, and thank you for sharing your story. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Cut. Thanks. I wanted to-- Ralph, who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is my grandfather and my grandmother from my mother's side. I believe their name was Lickman. His name was Abraham, and her name was Gittle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken in Moscow. I don't know when.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had these pictures when we were sent away from Mannheim to Gurs. She had these family pictures in her package, what she could take home 50--

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the pictures were from that package?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stay down. That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and my mother. My father was at that time 42 years old, and my mother was 21. And that is their wedding picture in Moscow in 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also my father. That picture was also taken in Moscow in 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Maxim Rafaelovich Lewin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, and that picture was taken in 1940 just before the deportation to Gurs. Her name is Anna, and her maiden name is Lickman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father just after his flight from Moscow to Riga. [INTERPOSING VOICES] And that was taken in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Rafael, in 1926, one-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Moscow. I'm here in Heidelberg in 1930 with a group of children just came over from Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it a school? Is it a classroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is just the kids what are living in my aunt's department or apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am-- I am on the left side in the front, the first little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and I, and I believe that this picture was taken in the same year that we came to Mannheim. And that must have been in 1940-- no, no, no-- '30, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is me, and that picture was taken in 1929 just before we-- my mother and I-- left to Riga. The picture was still taken in Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, and I was just 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mannheim, just a few months before the deportation. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is my best friend in Mannheim. His name is Albert Krechmer. His father was Lithuanian citizenship, and when we were deported, Lithuania was taken from Russia. And so he stayed in Mannheim, but later on, if the war started with Russia, he probably was deported, because I never found him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me. I believe that was taken in 1946, about close to a year after my liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in Paris, back in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you? How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old? Well, in '40, I must have been 21.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband to my right, and his name is Ralph Lewin. And behind him is my daughter, and her name is Sonia Litwack. And next to her, to-- to my back, is my son-in-law, and his name is Perry Litwack. And then we have Leah. That's my granddaughter, and her name is Leah Litwack. And this is my other granddaughter, and her name is Marina Litwack. And I'm Fanny Lewin. And now I'm going to give it to my daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to say that when we were younger, my siblings and I we-- we wanted to learn a lot about the Holocaust. And I know that it was difficult for my mom and my father to talk about it. And we've learned a lot through watching videos, and I was fortunate that I went to a Jewish school. So I learned a lot about my heritage and about what happened there, because it was difficult for them to talk about it. When I got a little older, I realized that they were able to speak a little bit more freely, but I know it was still very difficult. I want my children to know, so I'm trying to learn as much as possible so that at least what I have learned, I can pass on to them. And I'm-- I'm grateful that-- that things worked out and that my dad survived and my mom survived, and they were able to move to Canada, because I feel proud as a Canadian. And I'm glad that they picked a place that I feel quite safe to be living in. I-- I know that in my later years, I found out things about my mom and dad that I didn't even realize. And, of course, I couldn't even fathom, but I'm proud of them and-- and I'm grateful for them and my two kids. And I hope they have the opportunity being in a Hebrew school and learning their Jewish identity that they can pass on some of the things that my parents have gone through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can-- [INAUDIBLE]
